text: Hi, welcome to This is My Architecture. I'm Jennifer and I'm here with Nick from DISH Network.
Hi, Nick. Thanks for joining us today.
Hi, Jennifer. It's a pleasure to be here.
Can you tell us a little bit about DISH Network?
Absolutely. DISH Network Corporation is a connectivity company and a member of the Fortune 250.
DISH is currently building a cloud-based 5G open radio access network that will help run the smart cities of the future.
Excellent. Can you walk us through your architecture and how it's solving your business challenges today?
Absolutely. We needed an architecture that would help us run hundreds of accounts at scale.
It all starts with our IT service management tool.
Here, users can use a workflow to generate fully automated self-service AWS accounts.
Once the workflow approval conditions are met, the ITSM tool will kick off our account factory and control tower in our root account.
Here, a new AWS account is provisioned from scratch with all of our necessary account baselines in place.
Once this is done, the account is ready for any infrastructure that needs to be deployed into that account.
Typically, the first thing that needs to be done is networking.
So a VPC is provisioned into the vended account from our service catalog.

During the creation process, information about what environment and what resources that the infrastructure that lives in that VPC should connect to is collected and applied in the form of tags to the VPC.

When the VPC comes online, our service transferless transit network orchestrator, or SDNO, reads these tags and automatically creates a transit gateway attachment from that VPC to the transit gateway in our core network services account, providing network connectivity from that vended account to the other resources in the estate.
Great.
Great. So it seems to me that transit gateway is connecting your VPC to your central network services account, correct?
That's correct.
And how many of these accounts do you have connecting to the central services account?
With the efficiencies and benefits we were able to get with this and the self-service capabilities, we were able to roll out several hundred of these vended accounts in a space of only six months.
And eventually, when your VPCs are being deployed, how are you leveraging these for your internal stakeholders?
Once the VPC is deployed, our internal stakeholders are free to deploy whatever infrastructure they need to support the workloads they're running.
Frequently, they will turn to Service Catalog to continue to deploy other resources like EC2-based web apps, EKS-based web apps, and RDS databases.
Thank you, Nick, for walking us through this architecture.
It's been interesting to learn how DISH Network is creating a self-service portal to create multiple accounts at scale.
And thank you for watching.
This is my architecture.
Thank you.
